texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Mixing doubly stochastic quantum channels with the completely depolarizing channel
texts.text: Mixing doubly stochastic quantum channels with the completely depolarizing channel

texts.label: text
texts.orig: John Watrous
texts.text: John Watrous

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Institute for Quantum Computing and School of Computer Science University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, Canada.
texts.text: Institute for Quantum Computing and School of Computer Science University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, Canada.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is proved that every doubly stochastic quantum channel that is properly averaged with the completely depolarizing channel can be written as a convex combination of unitary channels. As a consequence, we find that the collection of channels expressible as convex combinations of unitary channels has non-zero Borel measure within the space of doubly stochastic channels.
texts.text: It is proved that every doubly stochastic quantum channel that is properly averaged with the completely depolarizing channel can be written as a convex combination of unitary channels. As a consequence, we find that the collection of channels expressible as convex combinations of unitary channels has non-zero Borel measure within the space of doubly stochastic channels.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let X and Y be finite-dimensional complex Hilbert spaces, and let L ( X ) and L ( Y ) denote the sets of linear operators acting on these spaces. By a super-operator one means a linear mapping of the form
texts.text: Let X and Y be finite-dimensional complex Hilbert spaces, and let L ( X ) and L ( Y ) denote the sets of linear operators acting on these spaces. By a super-operator one means a linear mapping of the form

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Such a super-operator is said to be admissible if and only if it is both completely positive and preserves trace. In the usual (Schr¨ odinger) picture of quantum information, the admissible superoperators represent valid physical operations, transforming a system having associated space X to one with associated space Y . For this reason, admissible super-operators are also commonly referred to as channels .
texts.text: Such a super-operator is said to be admissible if and only if it is both completely positive and preserves trace. In the usual (Schr¨ odinger) picture of quantum information, the admissible superoperators represent valid physical operations, transforming a system having associated space X to one with associated space Y . For this reason, admissible super-operators are also commonly referred to as channels .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A super-operator Φ is said to be unital if it is the case that Φ ( 1 X ) = 1 Y , for 1 X and 1 Y denoting the identity operators on X and Y , respectively. Super-operators that are both unital and admissible are said to be doubly stochastic . The existence of a doubly-stochastic channel of the form (1) obviously requires that X and Y have equal dimension d , which is assumed hereafter in this paper.
texts.text: A super-operator Φ is said to be unital if it is the case that Φ ( 1 X ) = 1 Y , for 1 X and 1 Y denoting the identity operators on X and Y , respectively. Super-operators that are both unital and admissible are said to be doubly stochastic . The existence of a doubly-stochastic channel of the form (1) obviously requires that X and Y have equal dimension d , which is assumed hereafter in this paper.

texts.label: text

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 1 The term random unitary channel is commonly used. However, this term is easily confused with a different notion that is now common in quantum information theory-that of a fi xed unitary operator that is randomly chosen according to some measure (frequently the Haar measure). For this reason, the term mixed-unitary channel is suggested in this paper.
texts.text: 1 The term random unitary channel is commonly used. However, this term is easily confused with a different notion that is now common in quantum information theory-that of a fi xed unitary operator that is randomly chosen according to some measure (frequently the Haar measure). For this reason, the term mixed-unitary channel is suggested in this paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the case d = 2 it holds that every doubly stochastic channel is mixed-unitary, but for larger d this is no longer the case: for all d ≥ 3 there exist doubly stochastic channels that are not mixedunitary [Tre86, KM87, LS93]. While it is computationally simple to check that a given channel is doubly stochastic, there is no efficient procedure known to check whether a given channel is mixed-unitary.
texts.text: For the case d = 2 it holds that every doubly stochastic channel is mixed-unitary, but for larger d this is no longer the case: for all d ≥ 3 there exist doubly stochastic channels that are not mixedunitary [Tre86, KM87, LS93]. While it is computationally simple to check that a given channel is doubly stochastic, there is no efficient procedure known to check whether a given channel is mixed-unitary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several papers have studied properties of mixed-unitary channels within the last several years. For instance, Gregoratti and Werner [GW03] gave a characterization, with an interesting physical interpretation, of the class of mixed-unitary channels: they proved that a given channel is mixed-unitary if and only if it has full quantum corrected capacity , which assumes that the correction procedure is permitted arbitrary measurements on the channel's environment. Smolin, Verstraete, and Winter [SVW05] further investigated this notion of capacity, and conjectured that many copies of any doubly stochastic channel can be closely approximated by a mixed-unitary channel. Buscemi [Bus06] investigated bounds on the number N required in the expression (2) for different channels; Audenaert and Scheel [AS08] investigated conditions under which channels are mixed-unitary; and Mendl and Wolf [MW08] proved several facts about mixed-unitary channels, including the fact that a non-mixed-unitary channel can become mixed-unitary when tensored with the completely depolarizing channel. Rosgen [Ros08] recently proved that various additivity questions about general channels reduce to the same questions on the (potentially simpler) class of mixed-unitary channels.
texts.text: Several papers have studied properties of mixed-unitary channels within the last several years. For instance, Gregoratti and Werner [GW03] gave a characterization, with an interesting physical interpretation, of the class of mixed-unitary channels: they proved that a given channel is mixed-unitary if and only if it has full quantum corrected capacity , which assumes that the correction procedure is permitted arbitrary measurements on the channel's environment. Smolin, Verstraete, and Winter [SVW05] further investigated this notion of capacity, and conjectured that many copies of any doubly stochastic channel can be closely approximated by a mixed-unitary channel. Buscemi [Bus06] investigated bounds on the number N required in the expression (2) for different channels; Audenaert and Scheel [AS08] investigated conditions under which channels are mixed-unitary; and Mendl and Wolf [MW08] proved several facts about mixed-unitary channels, including the fact that a non-mixed-unitary channel can become mixed-unitary when tensored with the completely depolarizing channel. Rosgen [Ros08] recently proved that various additivity questions about general channels reduce to the same questions on the (potentially simpler) class of mixed-unitary channels.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper proves a simple fact about mixed-unitary channels, which is that every doubly stochastic channel becomes mixed-unitary when properly averaged with the completely depolarizing channel. To state this fact more precisely, let us write Ω : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) to denote the completely depolarizing channel, which is defined as
texts.text: This paper proves a simple fact about mixed-unitary channels, which is that every doubly stochastic channel becomes mixed-unitary when properly averaged with the completely depolarizing channel. To state this fact more precisely, let us write Ω : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) to denote the completely depolarizing channel, which is defined as

texts.orig: It is also proved, as a corollary of this theorem, that within the smallest real affine subspace of super-operators that contains the doubly stochastic channels, there is a ball with positive radius around the completely depolarizing channel within which all super-operators are mixed-unitary channels.
texts.text: It is also proved, as a corollary of this theorem, that within the smallest real affine subspace of super-operators that contains the doubly stochastic channels, there is a ball with positive radius around the completely depolarizing channel within which all super-operators are mixed-unitary channels.

texts.text: The Choi-Jamiołkowski representation [Jam72, Cho75] of a super-operator Φ : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) is defined as

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mapping J defined in this way is a linear bijection from the space of all super-operators of the form L ( X ) → L ( Y ) to the space L ( Y ⊗ X ) . It holds that a super-operator Φ : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) is completely positive if and only if J ( Φ ) is positive semidefinite, trace-preserving if and only if Tr Y ( J ( Φ )) = 1 X , and unital if and only if Tr X ( J ( Φ )) = 1 Y . The following facts may be verified directly:
texts.text: The mapping J defined in this way is a linear bijection from the space of all super-operators of the form L ( X ) → L ( Y ) to the space L ( Y ⊗ X ) . It holds that a super-operator Φ : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) is completely positive if and only if J ( Φ ) is positive semidefinite, trace-preserving if and only if Tr Y ( J ( Φ )) = 1 X , and unital if and only if Tr X ( J ( Φ )) = 1 Y . The following facts may be verified directly:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. For any choice of an operator A ∈ L ( X , Y ) , the super-operator Φ : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) defined by Φ ( X ) = AXA ∗ satisfies J ( Φ ) = vec ( A ) vec ( A ) ∗ .
texts.text: For any choice of an operator A ∈ L ( X , Y ) , the super-operator Φ : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) defined by Φ ( X ) = AXA ∗ satisfies J ( Φ ) = vec ( A ) vec ( A ) ∗ .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. For any choice of operators A ∈ L ( X ) and B ∈ L ( Y ) , the super-operator Φ : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) defined by Φ ( X ) = 〈 A , X 〉 B satisfies J ( Φ ) = B ⊗ A .
texts.text: For any choice of operators A ∈ L ( X ) and B ∈ L ( Y ) , the super-operator Φ : L ( X ) → L ( Y ) defined by Φ ( X ) = 〈 A , X 〉 B satisfies J ( Φ ) = B ⊗ A .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When combined with the linearity of the mapping J , these facts will allow for simple calculations of J ( Φ ) for particular super-operators Φ appearing later in the paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Corollary 2 therefore establishes that within the smallest affine subspace that contains all such density operators, there is a ball of positive radius around the maximally mixed state containing only states that are expressible as mixtures of maximally entangled states. When expressed in these terms, it is appropriate to compare the results of the previous section with an analogous fact concerning the set of separable states, which are those expressible as mixtures of unentangled states.
texts.text: Corollary 2 therefore establishes that within the smallest affine subspace that contains all such density operators, there is a ball of positive radius around the maximally mixed state containing only states that are expressible as mixtures of maximally entangled states. When expressed in these terms, it is appropriate to compare the results of the previous section with an analogous fact concerning the set of separable states, which are those expressible as mixtures of unentangled states.

texts.orig: is a separable density operator. In contrast, Corollary 2 establishes that any unit-trace Hermitian operator ρ ∈ L ( Y ⊗ X ) satisfying both the linear constraints (8) and the bound
texts.text: is a separable density operator. In contrast, Corollary 2 establishes that any unit-trace Hermitian operator ρ ∈ L ( Y ⊗ X ) satisfying both the linear constraints (8) and the bound

texts.orig: is expressible as a convex combination of maximally entangled states. (It is clear that operators not satisfying the constraints (8) are not expressible as convex combinations of maximally entangled states, regardless of their distance from the maximally mixed state.)
texts.text: is expressible as a convex combination of maximally entangled states. (It is clear that operators not satisfying the constraints (8) are not expressible as convex combinations of maximally entangled states, regardless of their distance from the maximally mixed state.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is noted that the ball defined by (10) is properly contained in the one defined by (9). All of the states contained in the smaller ball are therefore representable at two extremes: as convex combinations of unentangled states and convex combinations of maximally entangled states. To what extent this fact can be generalized is an interesting question for future research.

All of the states contained in the smaller ball are therefore representable at two extremes: as convex combinations of unentangled states and convex combinations of maximally entangled states. To what extent this fact can be generalized is an interesting question for future research.
